content: A large language model (LLM) generates text by predicting one word at a time based on patterns it has learned from vast amounts of writing. It doesn’t “understand” language like a person but uses statistical relationships between words to guess what comes next. Imagine it like a super-advanced autocomplete: given a few words, it calculates which word is most likely to follow, then repeats this process to build sentences. The model has been trained on diverse texts—books, articles, websites—so it captures a wide range of language styles and facts. While it can produce convincing and coherent responses, it doesn’t have opinions or awareness; it simply mimics patterns it has seen before. This means it can sometimes generate incorrect or misleading information, so skepticism is healthy. Understanding this process helps to see why LLMs are powerful tools but not infallible sources of truth.
